sources.notes: Partition the sequence into the minimum number of subsequences whose adjacent values differ by exactly 1.
Official tutorial converts the task to a minimum path cover, then to bipartite matching, and gives a greedy matching proof plus Hall-DP variant.

annotation.summary: Split the sequence into as few subsequences as possible, where consecutive elements inside each subsequence differ by one.
annotation.constraints: Sum of n is 1e6, so the matching must be solved greedily or with a linear Hall-DP, not with a generic matching algorithm.
annotation.core_idea: A split into paths is a minimum path cover in the DAG of valid forward transitions i<j and |a_i-a_j|=1. By standard path-cover reduction, the answer is n minus the maximum matching size in the bipartite graph of positions. The graph separates by value parity; processing values from small to large and matching each occurrence to the closest available adjacent-value occurrence preserves optimality by an exchange argument.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) with ordered sets or O(n) with grouped positions.
tricks: minimum path cover to bipartite matching

solution_variants.name: greedy-bipartite-matching
solution_variants.summary: Match positions of value v greedily to nearest unused positions of value v+1 in the correct direction, do both directions/parities, and subtract matched edges from n.

solution_variants.name: hall-dp
solution_variants.summary: Use Hall's theorem on parity-separated value groups and DP over suffix choices for each value.

tags.evidence: The minimum number of subsequences equals n minus a maximum bipartite matching size.
The accepted main solution greedily chooses closest adjacent-value matches and proves it by exchange.
The path-cover reduction creates left and right copies of every position.
The editorial also presents a Hall-theorem DP alternative over grouped positions.
